item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually goddam beautiful. I love it that we see that the Foundation does have a heart, if ever so small that sometimes does the nice and good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely tearing up here, the Dickens quote got me
arguably one of my favorite heartwarming scps. merry whatever to anyone who reads this

item.title: Re: It's the most wonderful time of the year.
item.content:encoded:
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: U.S. Air Force
Licence: Public Domain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Siigh. It's hard to downvote something like this.

(This is all that truly needs to be said and is a Q.E.D. as far as the author might should be concerned imo, so I've included the rest of the comment in an optional collapsible below.)

+ show block
– hide block

It means well, the message is bright in a very dark universe, and you'd have to be an absolute Scrooge to not want something of this positivity to be on the site.

In addition, the anomaly — that is, the solution to the challenge of how to include Christmas and the traditions in a good-enough SCP article (admirable to tackle in its own right) — is clever and well-thought out in its fit. In opposition to others, I think it is the most successful part of the article. The end table is just dripping with Christmas spirit and truly is very touching.

So, I'm going to just refrain from an upvote and explain why.

A91 Active Intelligence Complex Model # N1-CK

This calls for either a (¬‿¬) or a (¬_¬). Leaning towards the latter myself, because it would mean a remarkable stroke of irony on the part of the 1980s military engineers and the tongue is pressing a bit too hard on the cheek from a compositional standpoint.

SCP-3355's character does a complete 180 around the time alexandra asks his name, and I don't really know why. The transition from curt and tight-lipped to gushing expose is not smoothed over well enough to overlook. It’s further bothersome because alexandra never actually tells him who she works for.

The "motherly figure cracks the asshole prickly personality with a heart of gold in wait for someone if only they put in the stubborn investment" is a pretty potent combination of cliches in an otherwise heartfelt and refreshing piece. It's like; I like what you've done with the place, but the decorative candle scent is giving me a headache. Lines like:

SCP-3355: Nick.

alexandra.aic: Huh? [When it just asked for a name.]

SCP-3355: Nick. My name is Nick.

(Brackets mine.)

…belong in a Matt-and-Trey satire of a holiday musical, but not a SCP article. It's an 11 on a 0-10 cringe scale, and is cheesier imo than a holiday hors d'oeuvres plate.

This is more of a personal nitpick, but the gratuitous profanity is a sour note for me in an otherwise wholesome article. I almost would wish the profanity was gone, and then the article could be an ambassador piece to the site and cross into being told or shared with family in Christmas cheer. Not the demographic this is written for, I know, but it otherwise comes so close.

Also, this St. Nick is not as good of a guy as first glance would lead one to believe. Sure, it's great that he's upset that a kid's parents aren't around the home and doesn't have a toy. But what about those in the greater Chicago area who are homeless or close to it? There has to be starvation looming in these same underprivileged areas, even some children who are going hungry. And instead of sending food or sheltering items to them, he sends toys to kids who are bummed about not having one? Only on one day of the year, despite it being the most magical? You know what's more magical than a toy on Christmas? Food and shelter, everyday. This operation is an absolute travesty given the SCP's capabilities and does not make Nick here good, or it doesn't cast him as intelligent enough to outsmart the Foundation at every turn.

The Foundation is absolutely not doing its job by letting this SCP do its thing. And I get that that's the emotional thrust; that even Foundation leadership as far-removed as the containment committee lead can have a soft heart for the real-life struggles of children in low socioeconomic conditions, but it is their freakin' job to prevent anomalies exactly like this from enacting their effect on the populace; whether good or bad. I would expect at least a little more time during the meetings over the huge philosophical debate this poses.

So the gut-punch of a last bit actually works against the article to me from an in-universe standpoint. And, this may be the kicker to boot, this part is abysmally justified with respect to the logic by the line about it outsmarting the Foundation, and is where all believability in this setup leaves.

In summary, there's just enough going on here that doesn't fit in-universe to where it negates the good feels that are done really well here. (For example, the part about the kid excitedly waking up only to find nothing is changed… really heartfelt feels and makes this believable, like the author has experienced this first-hand.)

In fairness, I'll say that I'm glad this article wasn't downvoted into deletion, and that I would have gladly added to its success, were this either toned down just a bit on the sentimentality, or I don't know maybe presented as a tale (where I feel some of the compromises made with believability from an in-universe Foundation perspective may have been softened just a bit).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't particularly like this. The description here is a setup, and then after that there's no delivery. It is pretty much what I'd expect it to be after reading the description. And that alone doesn't work for me in this case.

I suppose the fact that the Containment Committee is gleefully supporting of Nick is supposed to be some kind of punch, but that doesn't do anything for me either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Alright, let's get this over with. All in favor?"
"I"
"Great, meeting adjourned, have a nice weekend"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Never forget that the SCP Foundation always looks for the most efficient way to hide the existence of an SCP— and in this case, that's just setting up cover for it doing what it desires… helping others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could have easily fallen into the trap of being sentimental and saccharine, but it wasn't. It acknowledged how nasty the world is and was still somehow really nice regardless. The ending was just perfect. The whole thing was just nice. Really, really nice.

All the +1s. Good job, Kaktus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ignore, posting messed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This and its success are definitely driven by the narrative depth, which was well explored. If that were to be excluded, the core does seem to be relatively mundane of an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is classic Kaktus. To me, he's the best as balancing anomaly and mundanity. That's what I like most about this site. These kinds of articles are my favorite and I think Kaktus is the best at them.

I don't usually vote on articles because I don't want to only vote one way (I don't like downvoting) but I upvoted this. I didn't expect the Christmas theme, the description stuck the landing and didn't pull me out of the story, and the conclusion was what I was hoping for.

One criticism I could possibly give is that the article does try a little bit too hard to justify the Foundation allowing the AI to continue operation by making it "too advanced for us to stop!" But I personally forgive it because when you tie that in with the conclusion it doesn't disagree at all with the tone of the article and doesn't really matter that much.

TL:DR; Super neat article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it literally brought a tear to my eye. I cannot upvote this enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vote Meeting Length
(9)Maintain (0)Append Thirty-seven seconds.

This was probably my favourite part of the whole thing, and that's saying something. So much emotion and meaning in so few words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it, this reminds me of the Calculator or ZAX from Fallout with less malicious intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's to you, salty digital santa

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very likeable article. Who would have thought an AI military computer would send presents to poor families on Christmas? Thanks for the smile, here's your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Much like others, I already liked this, until I got to the bottom with the voting record.

As much as I love reading skips to feel unnerved and unsettled, sometimes I just like a happy ending.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… I'm just going to say that there are not a lot of SCPs that really… touched me deep inside. This is one of them. Truly.

I work in a state prison for kids and this really hit home because just the other day I was talking with some of the other folks that work here about how it must be just hitting home to the kids that they're not going to be with their families for Christmas. Now I read this and damn it all… I think I'm going to go against my better judgment and have a small Christmas party for them.

They may be a bunch of violent, angry, criminal rejects, but… they're my violent, angry, criminal rejects. And someone's got to offer them a different reality, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the way they go from a very long meeting to just voting for the maintenance of current protocols without hesitating. Great SCP, brought a smile to my face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
I really enjoyed this one, great work Kaktus.
One thing though, Fort Sheridan is not defunct, it now operates as an Army reserve center. Super nitpick-y I know, but the Fort is close to home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The next clay-mation Christmas Special: The AI that Saved Christmas.

Also, the amount of heart-warming shoved into one article. Who said SCPs had to be scary?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better yet: Christmas tale about this skip teaching SCP-079 the 'true meaning of Christmas'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this, then I saw the vote record and I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does this computer “see” kids waking up in the morning?

Also, I guarantee an upvote for someone who writes the tale that makes this the next SCP-1048.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am so relieved that the Overseers decided not to further fcuk with Sgt. Nick.

He's a chaotic good existentialist, who knows that sometimes the only time life gets better is when you force it to. He's doing all the good he can as long as he can, because he chooses to. He may be a brain in a box, but he's free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone in the Discovery addendum feels like it isnt quite clinical enough but otherwise this is a pretty likable SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my god kaktus I can't stop smiling. It's fantastic to see a ray of light amidst the wiki's grimdarkness, tragedy and despair. This made my year. Upvoted to heaven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting containment procedures, strong characterization, and a touching backstory all make this an easy +1 for me! Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy crap cacktus, this is touching. Nice to see something wholesome for a change instead of worldendingly dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know I wanted a robot military veteran character that is santa until now. Great stuff DeeJayCactus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey hey! You done did post it.

I really like this piece. I like the interactions between Alex and Nick, and I feel that you do a good job making this grizzled military AI into a sympathetic character.

While this may be a little sweeter than some of the fans of the darker Foundation will care for, this article makes me smile and is a refreshing change of pace from the usual paths of tragedy and bittersweetness that have become wiki staples.

+1 kaktus. Its beginning to look a lot like Christmas.

item.title: It's the most wonderful time of the year.
item.content:encoded:
I like Christmas, and not just because my birthday is coming up. Either way, here's something a little more wholesome for the holidays. No gloom and doom here, I promise.

Merry Christmas, SCP wiki.
